draft.introduction: 1 Corinthians 13 is Paul’s hymn to love (agape), arguably the most celebrated passage in the Pauline corpus. Placed between chapters on spiritual gifts, it redefines love as the supreme gift without which all others are meaningless.

translation.text: If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels, but have not love, I am a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal. Love is patient and kind; love does not envy or boast; it is not arrogant or rude. It does not insist on its own way; it is not irritable or resentful. Love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.
translation.original_text: ἐὰν ταῖς γλώσσαις τῶν ἀνθρώπων λαλῶ καὶ τῶν ἀγγέλων ἀγάπην δὲ μὴ ἔχω

translator_notes.note: Paul’s agape (ἀγάπη) is distinct from eros (desire) and philia (friendship). The Septuagint had already elevated agape as the term for God’s covenant love, and Paul here gives it its definitive Christian meaning as self-giving love.
